title: TypeScript Generics, Inference & Type-Level Programming
description: Two clusters in one quiz: **Generics & Inference** (when TS infers vs. when you must annotate, `extends` constraints, defaults, and why an unconstrained `<T>` leaks `unknown`) and **Type-Level Programming** (conditional types, `infer`, mapped & template-literal types). Read gnarly utility types and predict exactly what they resolve to. Every type-resolution claim was verified by compiling it with `tsc` 6.0 using `Expect<Equal<…>>` assertions.

questions.title: What type does `wrap("hi")` produce?
questions.explanation:
TypeScript infers `T` from the argument, but for a *plain* (non-`const`) type parameter a string-**literal value** is **widened** to its base type during inference. So `T` becomes `string`, not `"hi"`, and the return type `T[]` is `string[]`.

To keep the literal you have to opt in to a `const` context:

```ts
declare function wrap<const T>(value: T): T[];
const a = wrap("hi"); // now: "hi"[]
```

Widening is why a freshly inferred generic "loses" literal types unless you reach for `as const` or a `const` type parameter.

questions.title: With a `const` type parameter, what is the type of `x`?

options.text: `{ readonly name: string; readonly roles: readonly string[] }`

questions.explanation:
The `const` modifier on a type parameter (TS 5.0) makes inference behave **as if the caller wrote `as const`**: object properties become `readonly` and keep their literal types, and array literals become `readonly` *tuples* of literals.

So `name` is the literal `"Ada"` and `roles` is `readonly ["admin"]` — both `readonly`. Without the modifier the very same call widens to `{ name: string; roles: string[] }`. The two distractors that drop `readonly` or keep mutability are the half-right traps: `const T` always brings the `readonly` along.

questions.title: An uninferable type parameter *with a default* — what is `b`?
questions.description:
```ts
declare function makeBox<T = string>(): T[];

const b = makeBox(); // nothing is passed; T can't be inferred
```
questions.explanation:
When a type parameter has no inference site, TypeScript reaches for its **declared default**. There is no argument to infer `T` from, so `T` falls back to its default `string`, giving `string[]`.

This is the key contrast with the next idea: an *undeclared* default — a bare unconstrained `<T>` that can't be inferred — falls back to `unknown` (the "leaks `unknown`" behavior), **not** to a default. Defaults are how a library hands you a sensible type without forcing `makeBox<string>()` at every call site.

questions.title: What does `ToArray<string | number>` resolve to?
Does `Wrap<boolean>` collapse to `boolean[]`?
questions.explanation:
`boolean` is internally the union `true | false`. Because `Wrap` is a distributive conditional (naked `T`), it distributes over that union: `Wrap<true> | Wrap<false>` = `true[] | false[]`.

TypeScript does **not** re-collapse `true[] | false[]` into `boolean[]` — they are genuinely different types. `boolean[]` accepts a mixed array like `[true, false]`; `true[] | false[]` does not (every element would have to be all-`true` *or* all-`false`). This bites whenever a "harmless" helper is applied to `boolean` and the result no longer behaves like a plain `boolean[]`.

questions.title: A gnarly one: what is `R` when the same `infer U` sits in two parameter slots?
questions.explanation:
When one `infer U` appears in several positions, **variance decides how the candidates combine**. In *covariant* positions (like return types) TS takes the **union**; in *contravariant* positions — function **parameters** — it takes the **intersection**.

Here `x: infer U` is in two parameter slots, so `U = string & number`. No value is simultaneously a `string` and a `number`, so that intersection collapses to `never` — hence `R` is `never`.

Flip the methods to *return* the values and you get the union instead:

```ts
type Co<T> = T extends { a: () => infer U; b: () => infer U } ? U : never;
type R2 = Co<{ a: () => string; b: () => number }>; // string | number
```

questions.title: What shape does `Getters<{ name: string; age: number }>` produce?
questions.explanation:
Three features combine here. The mapped type iterates `K in keyof T`; the **`as` clause remaps each key** using a template-literal type; and `Capitalize<…>` upper-cases the first letter. `string & K` narrows `K` (which is `string | number | symbol`) down to `string` so the template will accept it. Each value type becomes `() => T[K]`.

Result: `{ getName: () => string; getAge: () => number }`. The distractors each drop one step — no `Capitalize`, no rename, or forgetting to wrap the value in `() => …`. Key remapping via `as` (TS 4.1) is also how you *filter* keys: map a key to `never` and it disappears from the result.

questions.title: Which statements about `NoInfer<T>` are true?

options.text: `NoInfer<C>` removes `def` as an *inference site*, so `C` is fixed by `colors` alone.

questions.explanation:
By default **every** argument typed `C` is an inference site. In `light`, the literal `"blue"` adds itself as a candidate, so `C` widens to `"red" | "green" | "blue"` and the call type-checks even though `"blue"` isn't a real color — a classic inference leak.

Wrapping the parameter in `NoInfer<C>` (TS 5.4) says *"use `C` here, but don't infer from this position."* Now `C` is pinned to `"red" | "green"` by the `colors` array and `"blue"` is correctly rejected. Like every type-level construct, `NoInfer` is erased during compilation and has **zero** runtime effect — so the claim that it changes the runtime value of `def` is false.

questions.title: Given this constrained `pluck`, which statements are true?
questions.explanation:
The constraint `K extends keyof T` ties `key` to the actual keys of `obj`. TS infers `T` as `{ id: number; name: string; active: boolean }`, so `keyof T` is `"id" | "name" | "active"`. Passing `"email"` violates the constraint, so it fails to compile *before the code ever runs*.

The return type `T[K]` is an **indexed access** — it resolves to the type stored at that exact key. Hence `"name"` ⇒ `string`, `"id"` ⇒ `number`, and `"active"` ⇒ `boolean` — not `string`, as the false statement claims. This `<T, K extends keyof T>` pairing is the backbone of type-safe getters, lodash-style `get`, and form libraries that keep field names and value types in lockstep.
questions.references: [TypeScript Handbook: Using Type Parameters in Generic Constraints](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/generics.html#using-type-parameters-in-generic-constraints)

questions.title: An unconstrained `<T>` that can't be inferred falls back to which type?
questions.explanation:
With no argument there is no inference site for `T`, and there is no declared default, so the compiler falls back to **`unknown`** — `r` has type `unknown`. This is the modern "`<T>` leaks `unknown`" behavior (very old TypeScript used the empty type `{}` instead).

It's a design smell: an unconstrained parameter that escapes into the return type usually means you should add a constraint (`<T extends …>`), supply a default (`<T = …>`), or annotate the call (`identity<number>()`). `unknown` is the safe top type — it can hold any value, but you must narrow it before you can use it.

questions.title: What does `Head<"a.b.c">` resolve to?
questions.description:
```ts
type Head<S extends string> = S extends `${infer H}.${infer _Rest}`
  ? H
  : S;

type R = Head<"a.b.c">;
```

Answer with the resolved string literal, written as its text without quotes.
questions.explanation:
Template-literal inference splits at the **first** occurrence of the literal delimiter. The pattern `` `${infer H}.${infer _Rest}` `` matches the first `.` in `"a.b.c"`, so `H` captures `"a"` and `_Rest` captures `"b.c"`. Therefore `R` is the literal type `"a"`.

Recurse on `_Rest` and you have a compile-time string splitter — the backbone of typed routers, i18n key checkers, and ORM path types. If the string contains no `.`, the conditional takes its false branch and returns `S` unchanged.

questions.title: Predict this recursive utility: what is `Flatten<number[][][]>`?
questions.explanation:
`Flatten` peels exactly one array layer per recursion:

```text
number[][][]  is Array<number[][]>  -> Inner = number[][]  (recurse)
number[][]    is Array<number[]>    -> Inner = number[]    (recurse)
number[]      is Array<number>      -> Inner = number      (recurse)
number        is NOT an array       -> false branch, return number
```

The recursion stops when the element is no longer an array, yielding `number`. It's a *deep* version of the one-level `type ElementType<T> = T extends (infer U)[] ? U : T`. Recursive conditional types like this are how `Awaited`, deep `Readonly`, and JSON-path utilities are built.
